title: keyId controls actor fetch target: attacker-supplied key injection
description:
server.rs:461: `let actor_uri = key_id.split('#').next().unwrap_or(&key_id);`

The actor_uri used to fetch the public key is derived entirely from the attacker-controlled keyId field in the Signature header. The actor_cache.get_public_key() fetches that URI and uses `publicKey.publicKeyPem` from the returned document.

actor_cache.rs:76 validates `publicKey.owner == actor_uri`, which prevents the simplest key-injection: attacker's server returns a document where owner matches the keyId base URI.

However, this still means:
- The attacker controls what host is fetched for the public key
- The attacker can register https://attacker.example/users/alice, serve any public key they control, and claim to be acting as that actor
- There is no validation that the actor_uri in the Signature header matches the `actor` field in the JSON body

An attacker with a valid key at attacker.example can sign as `keyId="https://attacker.example/users/alice#main-key"` while setting `"actor": "https://mastodon.social/users/realuser"` in the body. Signature verification passes (attacker.example key is valid and owner matches), and the Follow is inserted with `actor_uri = https://mastodon.social/users/realuser`.

Fix: after signature verification, assert that the actor_uri derived from keyId matches activity['actor'].
close_reason: All pentest fixes: require Signature header, verify Digest, enforce actor-keyId match, Date freshness, body size limit 256KB, webhook key via header, decompression bomb limits, symlink rejection, redirect following disabled, delivery SSRF recheck, backslash escape handling
